aliases: Tè zhì lā lā mǐ fěn

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for lala (clam) beehon. FOODON_00002572 (rice noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (rice noodles, cooked) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for clam-based rice vermicelli dishes in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific dish. SNOMED CT does not have granular codes for Filipino rice vermicelli dishes.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Special Lala Beehon is approximately 350 g, equivalent to one plate as typically served at a Filipino café in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Bee Hoon, Fried with Seafood — HPB Singapore Foodo / FCID composite; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central clam data (SR Legacy #15157) for shellfish contribution
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo provides fried bee hoon with seafood as the closest Singapore-specific analogue. Clam (lala) contribution cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy entry for raw clams (#15157) to verify protein and mineral plausibility. Values represent a weighted composite estimate for a dish of approximately 60% cooked rice vermicelli and 40% clam meat with sauce. Confidence is medium due to absence of an exact HPB entry for lala beehon specifically.

gi_source.title: Rice vermicelli, boiled — GI value 53 (Atkinson et al., 2008; University of Sydney GI Database)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lala beehon specifically. GI of 53 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled rice vermicelli (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Diabetes Care 2008). The clam and sauce components contribute negligible glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as: (53 × 53.9 g carbohydrate per 350 g serving) / 100 = 28.6. Carbohydrate per serving derived from per_100g value scaled to 350 g serving (15.4 × 3.5 = 53.9 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce, oyster sauce, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a standard serving and request reduced-sauce preparation, as a full 350 g serving provides approximately 1,470 mg sodium, representing 64–98% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 1,500–2,300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a standard 350 g serving carries a glycemic load of approximately 28.6 (high GL threshold ≥20); portion control to 150–200 g and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate dietary cholesterol from clams and cooking oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should note that the high sodium content and moderate cholesterol from clams (~98 mg per 350 g serving) warrant portion moderation; however, the low saturated fat content (0.7 g per 100 g) and presence of omega-3 fatty acids from clams are relatively favourable for lipid profiles.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Special Lala Beehon is a Filipino-style rice vermicelli dish featuring succulent lala clams (short-neck clams, Paphia undulata) stir-fried or braised with thin rice noodles in a savoury sauce typically made from garlic, ginger, soy sauce, oyster sauce, and chilli. The dish is popular in Filipino cafés and casual dining establishments in Singapore, where it is served as a main course. The rice vermicelli (beehon or beehoon) absorbs the briny, umami-rich clam juices and sauce, resulting in a flavourful, slightly chewy noodle base complemented by tender clam meat. Texturally, the dish combines soft noodles with the firm, slightly springy bite of fresh clam meat. Nutritionally, lala beehon provides a moderate energy density (~118 kcal per 100 g), meaningful protein from the clams (~7.2 g per 100 g), and notable micronutrients including iron (3.8 mg), vitamin B12 (1.8 mcg), selenium (18 mcg), and calcium (52 mg) — all contributed primarily by the shellfish component. The dish is high in sodium due to the seasoning sauces, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index of the rice vermicelli base is moderate-low (GI ~53), but the glycemic load per full serving is high (~28.6), requiring portion awareness for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not suitable for shellfish-allergic individuals and is not halal-certified in most preparations due to the use of non-halal oyster sauce variants.
common_user_queries: How many calories in lala beehon?
Is lala beehon high in sodium?
What are the nutrients in clam rice vermicelli?
What is beehoon with clams called?
Filipino noodle dish with clams Singapore

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists for Special Lala Beehon as a named dish. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB fried bee hoon with seafood as the closest Singapore-specific analogue, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy clam data. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice vermicelli. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level sources are approved and the dish composition is well-understood.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo fried bee hoon with seafood source entry, and clam vitamin D content is not consistently reported in USDA SR Legacy for this preparation. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source for this dish type. 3. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed zero as rice vermicelli and clams do not naturally contain industrial trans fats, and no hydrogenated oils are used in standard preparation — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy component data. 4. Sodium value of 420 mg per 100 g reflects a typical soy-sauce-seasoned preparation; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant. 5. Halal status is marked false as oyster sauce used in standard Filipino-style preparations may not be halal-certified; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 6. GI source is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (rice vermicelli, GI 53); no direct GI measurement for lala beehon exists in the peer-reviewed literature. 7. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy clam and rice noodle component data and should be treated as approximate.
